title: New Neighbours
summary: Maria and Dan meet for the first time in their apartment building. Dan has just moved in, and the two neighbours introduce themselves and share small details about their lives.

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: Oh, hi! I don't think we've met. I'm Maria — I live in flat twelve.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Hi Maria, I'm Dan. Nice to meet you. I just moved in last week, into flat fourteen.

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: Welcome to the building! How are you settling in?

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Pretty well, thanks. It's a bit quieter here than my last place, which is great.

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: Yes, it's usually quite peaceful. Where did you move from?

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: From the city centre. I wanted somewhere with a bit more space and a garden.

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: Oh, there's a small garden at the back. It's shared, but it's lovely in summer.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Oh, that's good to know! Do you use it much?

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: I do in summer. I sometimes have coffee out there in the morning. Are you working nearby?

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Yes, I work at the hospital. I'm a nurse. The commute is much easier from here.

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: That's great. I work from home actually, so it doesn't matter much where I live.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Oh, that sounds nice. What kind of work do you do?

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: I'm a graphic designer. I mostly work with small businesses on their logos and websites.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: That sounds like interesting work. Well, it was really nice to meet you, Maria.

body.speaker: Maria
body.text: You too! If you ever need anything — a cup of sugar, help with the bins — just knock on number twelve.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: That's very kind. I'll remember that. Thanks!

annotations.meaningVi: Tôi nghĩ chúng ta chưa gặp nhau
annotations.exampleEn: I don't think we've met before — are you new here?

annotations.meaningVi: thích nghi, quen với chỗ ở mới
annotations.exampleEn: It takes a few weeks to settle in after moving to a new city.
How is the new job going? Pretty well, thanks.

annotations.meaningVi: yên tĩnh hơn một chút so với
annotations.exampleEn: This neighbourhood is a bit quieter than the city centre.
The countryside is very peaceful in the evening.

annotations.phrase: somewhere with a bit more space
annotations.meaningVi: một nơi rộng rãi hơn một chút
annotations.exampleEn: We moved to find somewhere with a bit more space for the kids.

annotations.meaningVi: hay đấy, tốt là tôi biết được điều này
annotations.exampleEn: There's free parking at the back — good to know!

annotations.meaningVi: hành trình đi làm hàng ngày
annotations.exampleEn: His commute to work is only fifteen minutes by train.
Many people prefer to work from home two or three days a week.
She works as a graphic designer for an advertising agency.
If you need help, just knock on my door.

annotations.meaningVi: một tách đường (biểu tượng cho sự giúp đỡ hàng xóm)
annotations.exampleEn: Old neighbours used to borrow a cup of sugar from each other.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for recent events: 'I've just moved in'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has + past participle) dùng để nói về sự kiện vừa xảy ra gần đây, thường kết hợp với 'just'. Ví dụ: 'I just moved in last week' / 'I've just moved in.' Cả hai đều tự nhiên, nhưng 'just moved in' phổ biến hơn trong hội thoại bình thường.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) with 'just' to talk about very recent events. Example: 'I just moved in last week.' In British English, 'I've just moved in' is also common. Both forms are natural in everyday conversation.

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives with 'a bit': making softer comparisons
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thêm 'a bit' hoặc 'a little' trước tính từ so sánh hơn để làm cho sự so sánh nhẹ nhàng hơn. Ví dụ: 'a bit quieter', 'a bit more space'. Điều này giúp câu nói lịch sự và tự nhiên hơn trong hội thoại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Add 'a bit' or 'a little' before a comparative adjective to soften the comparison. Example: 'It's a bit quieter here than my last place.' / 'I wanted somewhere with a bit more space.' This is very common in polite, informal conversation.

translationVi:
Maria: Ồ, chào! Tôi không nghĩ chúng ta đã gặp nhau trước đây. Tôi là Maria — tôi sống ở căn hộ số mười hai.
Dan: Chào Maria, tôi là Dan. Rất vui được gặp bạn. Tôi vừa chuyển vào tuần trước, vào căn hộ số mười bốn.
Maria: Chào mừng đến với tòa nhà! Bạn đang thích nghi thế nào rồi?
Dan: Khá tốt, cảm ơn. Ở đây yên tĩnh hơn một chút so với chỗ cũ của tôi, điều đó thật tuyệt.
Maria: Đúng vậy, thường thì khá yên bình ở đây. Bạn từ đâu chuyển đến?
Dan: Từ trung tâm thành phố. Tôi muốn tìm một nơi rộng rãi hơn và có vườn.
Maria: Ồ, có một khu vườn nhỏ ở phía sau. Dùng chung, nhưng rất đẹp vào mùa hè.
Dan: Ồ, hay đấy! Bạn có hay ra đó không?
Maria: Tôi hay ra đó vào mùa hè. Đôi khi tôi uống cà phê ngoài đó vào buổi sáng. Bạn làm việc gần đây không?
Dan: Có, tôi làm việc tại bệnh viện. Tôi là y tá. Hành trình đi làm từ đây thuận tiện hơn nhiều.
Maria: Thật tuyệt. Tôi thì làm việc ở nhà, nên sống ở đâu cũng không quan trọng lắm với tôi.
Dan: Ồ, nghe hay đấy. Bạn làm công việc gì vậy?
Maria: Tôi là nhà thiết kế đồ họa. Tôi chủ yếu làm việc với các doanh nghiệp nhỏ về logo và website của họ.
Dan: Nghe có vẻ là công việc thú vị. Thôi, thực sự rất vui được gặp bạn, Maria.
Maria: Tôi cũng vậy! Nếu bạn cần bất cứ thứ gì — một tách đường, giúp đỡ với thùng rác — chỉ cần gõ cửa số mười hai nhé.
Dan: Bạn thật tốt bụng. Tôi sẽ nhớ điều đó. Cảm ơn!

questions.prompt: Which flat does Maria live in?
questions.explanation: Maria says 'I live in flat twelve.'
questions.hint: Maria introduces herself and her flat number at the start.

questions.prompt: When did Dan move into the building?
questions.explanation: Dan says 'I just moved in last week.'
questions.hint: Dan gives a specific time frame.

questions.prompt: How does Dan feel about his new home compared to his old place?
options: It is a bit quieter, which he likes
It is smaller, which he dislikes
It is the same as before
questions.explanation: Dan says 'It's a bit quieter here than my last place, which is great.'
questions.hint: Dan uses a comparative and adds his opinion.

options: A suburb on the north side
Another building on the same street
questions.hint: He wanted more space and less noise.

questions.prompt: What did Dan want from his new home?
options: A bigger bedroom and a garage
A bit more space and a garden
A shorter commute and a balcony
questions.explanation: Dan says 'I wanted somewhere with a bit more space and a garden.'
questions.hint: Two things are mentioned — space and an outdoor area.

questions.prompt: What does Maria say about the garden at the back?
options: It is private and only Maria uses it
It is shared but lovely in summer
It is being repaired at the moment
questions.explanation: Maria says 'It's shared, but it's lovely in summer.'
questions.hint: Two qualities of the garden are described.

questions.prompt: What does Maria sometimes do in the garden?
options: Has coffee there in the morning
questions.explanation: 'I sometimes have coffee out there in the morning.'
questions.hint: It is a morning activity involving a hot drink.

questions.explanation: Dan says 'I work at the hospital. I'm a nurse.'
questions.hint: He works at a hospital but is not a doctor.

questions.prompt: How has moving affected Dan's journey to work?
options: He now cycles instead of driving
He takes two buses instead of one
questions.explanation: 'The commute is much easier from here.'

questions.prompt: What does Maria do for work?
questions.hint: She works with logos and websites.

questions.prompt: What does Maria offer Dan at the end of the conversation?
options: Her phone number in case of emergencies
Help if he ever needs anything — knock on flat twelve
questions.explanation: She says 'If you ever need anything, just knock on number twelve.'
questions.hint: She gives a practical, friendly offer.

cloze.template:
Maria: Oh, hi! I don't think we've met. I'm Maria — I live in flat {{b1}}.
Dan: Hi Maria, I'm Dan. Nice to meet you. I just {{b2}} in last week, into flat fourteen.
Maria: Welcome to the building! How are you settling in?
Dan: Pretty well, thanks. It's a bit {{b3}} here than my last place, which is great.
Maria: Yes, it's usually quite {{b4}}. Where did you move from?
Dan: From the city {{b5}}. I wanted somewhere with a bit more space and a garden.
Maria: Oh, there's a small garden at the back. It's shared, but it's lovely in {{b6}}.
Dan: Oh, that's good to know! Do you use it much?
Maria: I do in summer. I sometimes have coffee out there in the morning. Are you working {{b7}}?
Dan: Yes, I work at the hospital. I'm a {{b8}}. The commute is much easier from here.
Maria: That's great. I work from {{b9}} actually, so it doesn't matter much where I live.
Dan: Oh, that sounds nice. What kind of work do you do?
Maria: I'm a graphic {{b10}}. I mostly work with small businesses on their logos and websites.
Dan: That sounds like interesting work. Well, it was really nice to meet you, Maria.
Maria: You too! If you ever need anything — a cup of sugar, help with the {{b11}} — just knock on number twelve.
Dan: That's very kind. I'll remember that. Thanks!

blanks.explanation: 'moved' — 'just moved in' means he relocated to the flat very recently.
'quieter' — Dan says the new place is a bit quieter than his last.
'peaceful' — Maria describes the building as usually quite peaceful.
'centre' — Dan moved from the city centre.
'summer' — the garden is lovely in summer.
'nearby' — Maria asks if Dan works nearby.
'nurse' — Dan works at the hospital as a nurse.
'home' — Maria works from home as a graphic designer.
'bins' — Maria offers to help with the bins, a common neighbourly task.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Maria and Dan's friendly conversation is the kind that can lead to a good neighbourly relationship. In modern cities, many people live in the same building for years without ever speaking to their neighbours. Why do you think this has become common, and what might we lose when we don't know the people who live near us?
